purpose: Legacy combined export (filesystem + story). Prefer campti-handoff-index.json and split artifacts for large uploads.

narrative.note: All ingestion is TypeScript run via tsx; there are no Python scripts in this repo.

prisma.note: PostgreSQL via DATABASE_URL; Prisma Client generated on postinstall/build.

characterProfiles.worldview: Kin and land move through language before they move through law; a name on paper is a door, not the whole house.
characterProfiles.desires: Carry letters and lineage without being reduced to a clerk’s spelling.
characterProfiles.fears: Paper that outlives the body and misnames the living.
characterProfiles.internalConflicts: Name as public handle versus corridor identity — translation as survival.
characterProfiles.notes: Chapter 1 POV — arrival and translation; demo character arc seed.

characterProfiles.worldview: The circle of adults is the shape of the world; trails and river are extensions of home.
characterProfiles.coreBeliefs: If the morning smoke rises, the day belongs to us.
characterProfiles.emotionalBaseline: Open curiosity with occasional unease when adults tighten without explaining.
characterProfiles.notes: POV lens only — not a psychologized modern child.

characterProfiles.worldview: Frontier as translation zone — languages, kinship, and paperwork overlap; survival is fluency across systems.
characterProfiles.internalConflicts: Loyalty split across kin, trade obligation, and colonial demand — exact emotional interior is partly speculative.
characterProfiles.notes: Bridge character for genealogical and thematic continuity toward Campti. Do not overstate uncertain ancestry or undocumented lines as settled fact. See private research source for excerpt.

characterProfiles.coreBeliefs: Continuity of family name and care across displacement.
characterProfiles.notes: Supporting bridge for lineage toward later generations; not meant to overload the opening Grande Terre scene.

characterProfiles.worldview: Rooted in kinship, land, continuity, and lived trade relationships — the world holds because people hold each other to it.
characterProfiles.coreBeliefs: The world is relational. Survival depends on memory, lineage, and reciprocal obligation — not on paper or distant decrees.
characterProfiles.desires: Continuity, safety, transmission of culture, preservation of dignity and belonging for those who come after.
characterProfiles.fears: Disruption of kinship lines; violent incursion; loss of daughters to chaos or capture; collapse of the order that keeps children safe.
characterProfiles.internalConflicts: How to survive inside a changing world without surrendering what makes a people themselves — adaptation without self-erasure.
characterProfiles.notes: Ambiguity around exact titles and formal tribal role is intentional. This profile is a narrative-world anchor, not a certificate of identity.
characterProfiles.personName: Marie Anne Thérèse de la Grande Terre

characterProfiles.worldview: Archives are acts of love when done with humility; the seam between oral and paper is sacred ground.
characterProfiles.coreBeliefs: Label uncertainty; refuse smooth AI overrides; protect living people from careless merges.
characterProfiles.notes: Demo narrator — continuity note on hidden identity relaxed for showroom clarity.

people.name: Child POV — Grande Terre (opening)
people.description: Narrative POV vehicle for pre-attack innocence and sensory immersion; not a historical individual.

people.name: Lillian Grappe
people.description: Frame narrator for the demo novel — archivist, merge reviewer, scene author; transparency over disguise.

people.name: Louise Marguerite Guédon
people.description: Genealogical bridge figure in the François Grappe research line; documentary presence varies — treat as hypothesis where records thin.

people.name: Marie Anne Thérèse de la Grande Terre
people.description: Origin matriarchal anchor for the opening world — inferred social position; formal title and exact role in community governance intentionally left ambiguous.

places.name: Campti — cemetery corridor
places.description: Racially stratified burial ground — represented in fiction with ethical restraint; not flattened into metaphor.

places.name: Cane River corridor
places.description: Parishes and kin paths between Natchitoches and Campti.

places.name: Grande Terre Homeland Zone
places.description: Trade-route homeland / village zone before rupture — composite setting for early-contact rhythm (not a single surveyed parcel).

places.name: Grappe kitchen / home archive
places.description: Roux, okra, letters on table — domestic method.

places.name: Natchitoches landing
places.description: River stairs, trade street, clerk offices — arrival grammar.

themes.name: Anchored time
themes.description: Historical anchors constrain fantasy; when years or events are known, honor them in scene construction.

themes.name: Hybridity as method
themes.description: When oral and documentary strands disagree, label the seam — fragments are not facts by default.

themes.name: Land as memory vs land as file
themes.description: Cession, corridor, return — paper maps and walked paths in tension.

themes.name: Memory vs record
themes.description: Emotional truth and documented fact can diverge; both remain legible in the model as separate layers.

themes.name: Place as witness
themes.description: Landscape and town hold memory that outlives individual narrators; setting is an active carrier of meaning.

motifs.name: Smoke on the corridor
motifs.description: Winter fields, chimneys, signal — memory before explanation.
motifs.usagePattern: Reprise at chapter turns; sensory return hook.

narrativeRules.title: Label oral history distinctly
narrativeRules.description: Oral claims (e.g., injury stories) remain flagged hybrid until documentary confirmation — never silent merge.

narrativeRules.title: Motifs earn recurrence
narrativeRules.description: Recurring images should accrue meaning across scenes rather than repeating as decoration.

narrativeRules.title: Nonlinear truth is allowed
narrativeRules.description: The overview establishes that the narrative may move across time and teller; the system must not force a single chronological spine as the only valid reading.

narrativeRules.title: Preserve interpretive gaps
narrativeRules.description: Do not collapse ambiguous family or historical material into a single authoritative reading in generation prompts.

literaryDevices.name: Seam revelation
literaryDevices.description: Direct address in epilogue; second-person ethics; UI metaphor for merge.
literaryDevices.systemEffect: Forces reader awareness of construction without breaking immersion carelessly.

narrativePatterns.title: Generational echo
narrativePatterns.description: Alexis → François → Buford → Lillian — same seam, different register.

narrativePatterns.title: Lineage shadows present action
narrativePatterns.description: Present-day choices echo generational patterns; genealogy informs subtext, not exposition dumps.

narrativePatterns.title: Oral cadence inheritance
narrativePatterns.description: Dialogue and interior monologue may carry oral-storytelling rhythm even in close third.

fragments.title: Merge review note
fragments.text: Are we sure this François is that François? Split the knot.

fragments.title: Seam log (fiction)
fragments.text: Where strands disagree, keep both — label the seam.

fragments.title: Adult unease before violence names itself
fragments.text: Someone laughs late; someone else doesn’t. A question hangs a half-beat too long. The child notices only that the day feels ordinary.

fragments.title: Child certainty the world will continue
fragments.text: The child does not doubt tomorrow — doubt is an adult tool. Tomorrow is a shape adults build while the child plays inside it.

fragments.title: Elder women as the system
fragments.text: The elder women do not ‘help’ the village — they are its memory engine: who owes what, who married in, who cannot be trusted with fire.

fragments.title: François Grappe — research bridge (not the opening emotional center)
fragments.summary: Explicit deferral of surname dominance; bridge to later generations.
fragments.text: The Grappe name will arrive later with documentation and dispute — here, at Grande Terre, the world is still named by mothers, water, and smoke.

fragments.title: Maternal order (narrator register)
fragments.summary: Frame opening as maternal / communal order.
fragments.text: We begin where a mother’s hands still sort the world into knowable pieces — before history arrives with its louder tools.

fragments.title: Peace must be made real
fragments.summary: Moral stake: peace as active creation.
fragments.text: Peace is not the absence of noise — it is the agreement, renewed daily, that we will not turn on each other first.

fragments.title: River and trail knowledge — embodied
fragments.text: Nobody explains the ford; bodies explain it — how cold, where the slick is, when the season makes the bank a lie.

fragments.title: Morning smoke — continuity without speech
fragments.summary: Smoke as proof of ongoing life — sensory anchor for peace.
fragments.text: Smoke rises at the hour it is supposed to, and the child reads that as law: if smoke, then day; if day, then we continue.

fragments.title: The trail as familiar artery
fragments.summary: Trade civilization as relationship, not abstract ‘frontier.’
fragments.text: The trade route is not danger yet — it is the village’s widened breath: people you might love, goods you might need, stories carried in dust.

fragments.title: Names and roles through women
fragments.text: Titles slip across languages; what persists is who remembers your mother’s line when the paper does not.
Oral accounts describe Buford Grappe losing his arm in a hunting accident; treat as hybrid until parish records confirm.

fragments.summary: Sensory memory tied to communal gathering.
fragments.text: Smoke on winter fields marked gatherings — memory before explanation.

fragmentClusters.title: Grande Terre — pre-loss calm
fragmentClusters.summary: Peace as rhythm: trade, women’s work, child certainty.

fragmentClusters.title: Paper trail / oral river
fragmentClusters.summary: Chapters 4 & 9 — ingestion, merge fight, seam log.

fragmentClusters.title: Smoke / memory / continuity
fragmentClusters.summary: Sensory and moral images of smoke as continuity before rupture.

fragmentClusters.title: Smoke → lineage → return
fragmentClusters.summary: Signal/memory across generations; ties scenes to symbol seed-smoke.

sources.title: François Grappe and the Caddo Land Cession – Dayna Bowker Lee excerpt
sources.summary: Research excerpt describing François Grappe as a mixed French-Indigenous frontier figure born in 1747 near the Caddo post by present-day Texarkana, educated by Alexis Grappe, embedded with Caddo communities, and active across French, Spanish, and later U.S. frontier systems.

sources.title: Grappe Legacy: A Comprehensive Guide
sources.summary: Compiled family notes, land references, and narrative hooks for the Campti–Natchitoches corridor.

sources.title: Section IX — Subthemes, Motifs, and Literary Devices
Section V — Narrative Structure & Chapter Frameworks
Section XI — Theological and Spiritual Symbolism
Section XIII — Enneagram Mapping of Primary Characters
Section XIV — Symbolism and Recurring Motifs
Section XVI — Literary and Visual Symbolism Guide
The Smoke of the Corridor — Demo manuscript spine
sources.summary: Synthetic spine document for narrative DNA bindings in showroom.

claims.description: DNA percentages are hypotheses about affinity, not tribal enrollment certificates.
Reservation discussed in kitchens along the corridor before appearing in indexed files.

openQuestions.title: What is the strongest evidence trail for the Grappe Reservation treaty?
openQuestions.description: Map citations and oral history against documentary fragments.

openQuestions.title: Was François Grappe Jr. formally documented in surviving records?
openQuestions.description: Track parish, federal, and tribal records; note gaps explicitly.

openQuestions.title: Is every cinematic pass ready for public?
openQuestions.description: Spot-check published passes before wide demo.

openQuestions.title: How should the graveyard racial dividing line be represented in narrative vs documentation?
openQuestions.description: Resolve ethics of depiction; avoid flattening violence into metaphor.

openQuestions.title: What defines strength in the Grappe lineage?
Where does memory diverge from truth?
What is inherited vs chosen identity?
Where does faith heal vs harm?
Who truly holds power in the family?
What parts of this timeline are oral vs documented?
Where does identity shift legally vs culturally?
What was lost during each transition?

continuityNotes.title: Narrator reveal must remain hidden until final chapter
continuityNotes.description: Structure scenes so identity clues are reversible until the close.

continuityNotes.title: Track whether Buford’s accidents are documented, oral, or hybrid
continuityNotes.description: Align Buford’s arc with record types and claims.

continuityNotes.title: Showroom: narrator identity public in epilogue
continuityNotes.description: Continuity from early seed relaxed for demo — re-tighten narrator concealment for production novel if desired.

continuityNotes.title: Do not collapse oral history into verified fact without labeling
continuityNotes.description: Use chapter notes and claim types to keep hybridity visible.

metaScenes.title: Grande Terre — Three Days Before the Attack
metaScenes.timePeriod: Early 1700s — before French violence reaches the immediate scene (approximate)
metaScenes.narrativePurpose: Make the reader love the world before it breaks — emotional foundation for the entire novel.
metaScenes.symbolicElements: Smoke as continuity; river/trail as embodied knowledge; women’s labor as civilization; child’s certainty as tragic irony.
metaScenes.centralConflict: Peace as earned equilibrium vs. forces that treat land and people as disposable — violence is not yet present, but the story’s moral stakes are named.
metaScenes.emotionalVoltage: High warmth with a thin wire of dread — the reader should love the world before learning its fragility.

characterRelationships.relationshipSummary: Child POV orbits Marie’s watchful calm; safety assumed until adult tension sharpens without full explanation.
